title: ACAS Guide: Early Conciliation and Dispute Resolution
topicLabel: Understanding early conciliation before making a tribunal claim
chapters: ACAS Early Conciliation · Mandatory pre-claim process since 6 May 2014 · Governed by ERA 1996 s.18A–18C (inserted by Enterprise and Regulatory Reform Act 2013 s.7) · Employment Tribunals (Early Conciliation: Exemptions and Rules of Procedure) Regulations 2014 (SI 2014/254)
desc: ACAS Early Conciliation is the mandatory pre-claim process that a prospective claimant must undertake before presenting a claim to an employment tribunal. Introduced on 6 May 2014 by the Enterprise and Regulatory Reform Act 2013, it requires the prospective claimant to contact ACAS before issuing proceedings. ACAS then offers to conciliate between the parties with the aim of resolving the dispute without the need for a tribunal hearing. If conciliation is unsuccessful or the respondent declines to participate, ACAS issues a certificate which the claimant must present when submitting their ET1 form. The Early Conciliation period is up to one calendar month (extendable by a further 14 days with consent). The limitation period for bringing a tribunal claim is paused ('stopped') during the Early Conciliation period. ACAS conciliation is free, voluntary (in the sense that the parties cannot be compelled to settle), and confidential. Anything said during conciliation is inadmissible in subsequent tribunal proceedings. ACAS also provides voluntary conciliation at any stage of tribunal proceedings and offers mediation, arbitration, and collective conciliation services for workplace disputes.
legalStatus: Early Conciliation is a mandatory statutory pre-claim requirement. A tribunal claim cannot be accepted without an Early Conciliation certificate (or a valid exemption). The conciliation service itself is governed by TULRCA 1992 s.210 and ERA 1996 s.18A–18C.

sections.title: The mandatory pre-claim process
sections.summary: Before presenting most employment tribunal claims, the prospective claimant must contact ACAS by submitting an Early Conciliation notification form (online or by telephone). ACAS will then contact both parties and offer to conciliate. The Early Conciliation period is one calendar month from the date ACAS receives the notification. ACAS may extend this by a further 14 days if both parties consent and ACAS considers there is a reasonable prospect of achieving a settlement. If settlement is not reached, ACAS issues an Early Conciliation certificate. The claimant must include the certificate number on their ET1 form. There are limited exemptions from the requirement, including where the claim is brought against the Security Service, SIS, or GCHQ, or where the claim involves an interim relief application. The limitation period for bringing a tribunal claim is 'stopped' during the Early Conciliation period (the period from when ACAS receives the notification to when the certificate is issued does not count towards the time limit). An additional calendar month is added to the limitation period if it would otherwise expire during the Early Conciliation period or within one month of receipt of the certificate.
sections.text:
Before presenting a claim to an employment tribunal, the prospective claimant must contact ACAS and provide prescribed information about the prospective claim and the parties involved.

ACAS will then:
• contact both parties to offer conciliation
• explore whether there is any prospect of reaching a settlement
• if settlement is possible, facilitate negotiations between the parties
• if settlement is not reached within the EC period, issue an Early Conciliation certificate

The EC period is one calendar month from the date ACAS receives the notification. This may be extended by up to 14 days if:
• both parties consent; and
• ACAS considers there is a reasonable prospect of settlement

The limitation period for tribunal claims is 'stopped' during the EC period. The period from ACAS receiving the notification to issuing the certificate does not count towards the time limit.

If the time limit would expire during the EC period or within one month of the certificate being issued, the time limit is extended to one month after the certificate is received.

Anything communicated during Early Conciliation is confidential and inadmissible in subsequent tribunal proceedings.

versions.description: Early Conciliation introduced on a voluntary basis for prospective claimants.

Early Conciliation became mandatory for all prospective tribunal claimants (with limited exemptions). Employment Tribunals (Early Conciliation: Exemptions and Rules of Procedure) Regulations 2014 came into force.

leadingCases.name: Science Warehouse Ltd v Mills [2016] ICR 252
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT held that the names on the Early Conciliation certificate must correspond with the names on the ET1 form. A claim against a respondent not named on the certificate may be rejected. However, minor discrepancies in naming (e.g. trading name versus legal name) may be excused where it is clear that the same entity is intended.
leadingCases.significance: Key authority on the requirement for correspondence between EC certificate and ET1 naming

leadingCases.name: Mist v Derby Community Health Services NHS Trust [2016] ICR 543
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT provided comprehensive guidance on the calculation of time limits where Early Conciliation applies. The 'stop the clock' provisions require careful calculation to determine the actual deadline for presenting a claim.
leadingCases.significance: Authoritative guidance on time limit calculation under the Early Conciliation regime

refs: ERA 1996 s.18A (requirement to contact ACAS before instituting proceedings)
Enterprise and Regulatory Reform Act 2013 s.7 (inserted ss.18A–18C into ERA 1996)
Employment Tribunals (Early Conciliation: Exemptions and Rules of Procedure) Regulations 2014 (SI 2014/254)

sections.title: COT3 agreements: binding settlements through ACAS
sections.summary: Where settlement is reached through ACAS conciliation (either during Early Conciliation or at any stage of tribunal proceedings), the terms are recorded on a COT3 form. A COT3 agreement is a binding legal agreement that prevents the employee from pursuing the tribunal claim. Unlike a settlement agreement under ERA 1996 s.203(3), a COT3 does not require the employee to have received independent legal advice — the involvement of ACAS is itself sufficient to meet the statutory requirements for contracting out of employment rights. A COT3 can settle claims that have not yet been brought, provided the conciliation officer has been involved. The terms of a COT3 are enforceable as a contract. Where a COT3 provides for a payment, the payment is normally tax-free up to £30,000 (subject to ITEPA 2003 s.403).
sections.text:
Where the parties reach a settlement through ACAS conciliation, the terms are recorded on a COT3 agreement. The COT3 is signed by both parties and the ACAS conciliation officer.

A COT3 agreement:
• is a binding legal agreement enforceable as a contract
• prevents the employee from pursuing the tribunal claim(s) covered by the agreement
• does not require the employee to have received independent legal advice (unlike a settlement agreement under ERA 1996 s.203(3))
• can settle claims that have been brought to the tribunal or claims that have not yet been brought
• is only valid if an ACAS conciliation officer has taken action under TULRCA 1992 s.210 or ERA 1996 s.18A

The distinction between a COT3 and a settlement agreement is important:
• COT3: requires ACAS involvement; no independent legal advice needed; covers all types of employment claims
• Settlement agreement: does not require ACAS involvement; requires independent legal advice from a relevant independent adviser; must comply with ERA 1996 s.203(3) requirements

leadingCases.name: Byrnell v British Telecommunications plc [2017] UKEAT/0383/16
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT considered the scope of a COT3 agreement and held that a COT3 can validly settle claims that have not yet been brought, provided the conciliation officer acted under the statutory conciliation powers. The terms of the COT3 determine the scope of the settlement.
leadingCases.significance: Confirmed that COT3 agreements can validly settle future claims if properly drafted

leadingCases.name: Telefonica UK Ltd v O'Neill [2021] UKEAT/0061/20
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT considered whether a COT3 agreement reached through ACAS conciliation was binding where the claimant alleged they had been pressured into accepting the terms. The EAT held that a COT3 is a contract and the normal rules of contract law (including duress and undue influence) apply. However, the involvement of ACAS provides a significant safeguard and the bar for setting aside a COT3 is high.
leadingCases.significance: Clarified that COT3 agreements are contracts subject to normal contractual vitiating factors, but the ACAS involvement provides a high threshold for challenge

refs: ERA 1996 s.203(2)(e) (COT3 as exception to the prohibition on contracting out)
ITEPA 2003 s.403 (tax treatment of termination payments — first £30,000 exempt)

sections.title: Mediation, arbitration, and collective conciliation
sections.summary: ACAS provides a range of dispute resolution services beyond Early Conciliation. Mediation is a voluntary process where an independent mediator helps the parties reach their own resolution. ACAS offers workplace mediation for individual disputes (e.g. between colleagues, between employee and manager) and can also provide mediation in discrimination and whistleblowing cases. Arbitration under the ACAS Arbitration Scheme provides a binding alternative to employment tribunal proceedings for unfair dismissal and flexible working claims. The arbitrator's decision is final and there is no right of appeal on a point of law (unlike tribunal decisions). Collective conciliation is provided where there are disputes between employers and trade unions, including disputes about recognition, pay, terms and conditions, redundancy, and disciplinary matters. ACAS collective conciliation is free and confidential.
sections.text:
ACAS provides the following dispute resolution services:

Mediation:
• voluntary process where an independent mediator helps parties reach their own resolution
• suitable for relationship disputes, bullying complaints, team conflicts, and communication breakdowns
• mediator does not impose a decision — the parties control the outcome
• confidential — what is said in mediation cannot be used in subsequent proceedings

ACAS Arbitration Scheme:
• binding alternative to employment tribunal for unfair dismissal and flexible working claims
• hearing is private (unlike tribunal which is public)
• arbitrator's decision is final — no appeal on point of law
• both parties must agree to use the scheme
• governed by the ACAS Arbitration Scheme (Great Britain) Order 2004 (SI 2004/753)

Collective Conciliation:
• free service for disputes between employers and trade unions
• ACAS conciliator facilitates negotiations to reach agreement
• covers disputes about recognition, pay, terms, redundancy, and disciplinary matters
• governed by TULRCA 1992 s.210

versions.description: ACAS Arbitration Scheme for unfair dismissal claims first introduced.
ACAS Arbitration Scheme (Great Britain) Order 2004 replaced the original 2001 scheme. Extended to flexible working claims.

pendingAmendments: ERA 2025 — Fair Work Agency may bring tribunal claims on behalf of workers (not yet in force)
EC process unchanged — mandatory since May 2014

parliamentaryOrigin.statutoryBasis: Statutory Framework — ERA 1996 ss.18A-18C (as inserted by Enterprise and Regulatory Reform Act 2013 s.7)
parliamentaryOrigin.legalEffect: Mandatory Early Conciliation became compulsory from 6 May 2014. Prospective claimants must contact ACAS before lodging an employment tribunal claim. ACAS issues an Early Conciliation certificate which must be presented with the tribunal claim. The conciliation period pauses the limitation clock.
parliamentaryOrigin.note: ACAS conciliation (both voluntary under TULRCA 1992 s.210 and mandatory Early Conciliation under ERA 1996 s.18A) is the primary dispute resolution mechanism before tribunal proceedings. COT3 settlements reached through ACAS conciliation are binding and are one of only two methods of validly settling employment tribunal claims (the other being a settlement agreement under ERA 1996 s.203(3)).

precedingDocuments.title: Enterprise and Regulatory Reform Act 2013 s.7
precedingDocuments.significance: Inserted ERA 1996 ss.18A-18C establishing mandatory Early Conciliation from 6 May 2014.

precedingDocuments.title: TULRCA 1992 s.210
precedingDocuments.significance: The longstanding statutory basis for voluntary ACAS conciliation. EC operates alongside this voluntary framework.

leadingCases.name: Sterling v United Learning Trust [2015] UKEAT/0439/14
leadingCases.heldText: The EAT considered the Early Conciliation requirement and held that failure to obtain an EC certificate before presenting a tribunal claim renders the claim invalid.
leadingCases.significance: EC certificate is a mandatory prerequisite — claims without it are invalid.

leadingCases.name: Compass Group UK & Ireland Ltd v Morgan [2016] IRLR 924
leadingCases.heldText: The EC certificate must name the correct respondent. A claim against a different entity from that named in the EC certificate may be rejected.
leadingCases.significance: EC certificate must identify the correct respondent.
